title: HSE Guidance: Employer Health and Safety Duties
chapters: HSE regulatory guidance · Covers Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974 ss.2–9 · Management of Health and Safety at Work Regulations 1999 · Risk assessment, safe systems of work, training, consultation, reporting obligations
desc: The Health and Safety Executive (HSE) publishes comprehensive guidance on employer duties under the Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974 (HSWA) and subordinate regulations. HSWA is the primary legislation governing occupational health and safety in Great Britain. Section 2 imposes a general duty on employers to ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, the health, safety and welfare at work of all employees. Section 3 extends the duty to non-employees who may be affected by the employer's undertaking. Section 7 imposes duties on employees. The Act is enforced by the HSE and local authorities through improvement notices, prohibition notices, and criminal prosecution. Penalties are unlimited fines and, for individuals, custodial sentences. The Management of Health and Safety at Work Regulations 1999 (MHSWR) add detailed requirements including risk assessment (reg.3), health and safety arrangements (reg.5), health surveillance (reg.6), competent assistance (reg.7), and information and training (regs.10–13). For the Northerly Hill demo entity operating in facilities management, HSE compliance is operationally critical across all depot locations.
legalStatus: HSE guidance is authoritative but non-statutory. However, Approved Codes of Practice (ACOPs) issued by the HSE have a special legal status: failure to follow an ACOP is not in itself an offence, but courts may take the failure into account as evidence that a statutory requirement has been contravened, unless the employer can demonstrate compliance by an equally effective method (HSWA s.17).

sections.title: General duties of employers to their employees
sections.summary: Every employer must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, the health, safety and welfare at work of all employees. This general duty is expanded by specific obligations: provision and maintenance of plant and systems of work that are safe and without risks to health; arrangements for ensuring safety in the use, handling, storage and transport of articles and substances; provision of information, instruction, training and supervision; maintenance of the workplace including access and egress; and provision and maintenance of a working environment that is safe, without risks to health, and adequate as regards welfare facilities. Employers with 5 or more employees must prepare a written health and safety policy (s.2(3)). Employers must consult with safety representatives and, if requested by recognised trade union safety representatives, establish a safety committee (s.2(7)).

versions.description: Original provision. Royal Assent 31 July 1974. Commencement 1 April 1975. Based on recommendations of the Robens Committee Report 1972.

leadingCases.name: Edwards v National Coal Board [1949] 1 KB 704
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal defined 'reasonably practicable' as a narrower term than 'physically possible'. It involves weighing the quantum of risk against the sacrifice (in time, trouble, and expense) involved in averting the risk. If the risk is insignificant in relation to the sacrifice, the employer need not take the precaution.
leadingCases.significance: The definitive authority on the meaning of 'reasonably practicable' — the central concept of UK health and safety law

leadingCases.name: R v Associated Octel Co Ltd [1996] UKHL 1
leadingCases.heldText: The House of Lords held that an employer's duty under section 3 extends to the activities of independent contractors where those activities form part of the employer's undertaking. The test is whether the activity is part of the employer's conduct of their undertaking, not whether the injured person is an employee.
leadingCases.significance: Extended employer duties to contractor activities forming part of the employer's undertaking — critical for FM sector

leadingCases.name: R v Tangerine Confectionery Ltd [2011] EWCA Crim 2015
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal confirmed that the burden of proving that it was not reasonably practicable to do more rests on the employer. The prosecution need only prove the risk; the employer must then demonstrate that they did all that was reasonably practicable to address it.
leadingCases.significance: Confirmed the reverse burden of proof in health and safety prosecutions — employer must prove reasonable practicability

refs: HSWA 1974 s.4 (duties of persons in control of premises)
HSWA 1974 s.37 (offences by bodies corporate — director liability)
Management of Health and Safety at Work Regulations 1999 reg.3 (risk assessment)

sections.title: General duties of employers to persons other than their employees
sections.summary: Every employer must conduct their undertaking in such a way as to ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that persons not in their employment who may be affected by it are not thereby exposed to risks to their health or safety. Self-employed persons have an equivalent duty. This section is critical for facilities management employers who have contractors, subcontractors, visitors, and members of the public on their premises.
sections.text:
(1) It shall be the duty of every employer to conduct his undertaking in such a way as to ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that persons not in his employment who may be affected thereby are not thereby exposed to risks to their health or safety.

(2) It shall be the duty of every self-employed person to conduct his undertaking in such a way as to ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that he and other persons (not being his employees) who may be affected thereby are not thereby exposed to risks to their health or safety.

(3) In such cases as may be prescribed, it shall be the duty of every employer and every self-employed person, in the prescribed circumstances and in the prescribed manner, to give to persons (not being his employees) who may be affected by the way in which he conducts his undertaking the prescribed information about such aspects of the way in which he conducts his undertaking as might affect their health or safety.

leadingCases.name: R v Board of Trustees of the Science Museum [1993] 1 WLR 1171
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that a risk of harm to a member of the public is sufficient for a prosecution under section 3. It is not necessary to prove that anyone was actually harmed — the offence is the exposure to risk.
leadingCases.significance: Confirmed that exposure to risk alone is sufficient for prosecution — actual harm need not be proved

refs: HSWA 1974 s.4 (duties of persons in control of premises)
Construction (Design and Management) Regulations 2015 (CDM — construction sector duties)

sections.title: General duties of employees at work
sections.summary: Every employee while at work must take reasonable care for the health and safety of themselves and of other persons who may be affected by their acts or omissions at work. Employees must cooperate with their employer so far as is necessary to enable the employer to perform their duties under the Act. It is a criminal offence for an employee to fail to comply with these duties.
sections.text:
(a) It shall be the duty of every employee while at work—
to take reasonable care for the health and safety of himself and of other persons who may be affected by his acts or omissions at work; and
(b) as regards any duty or requirement imposed on his employer or any other person by or under any of the relevant statutory provisions, to co-operate with him so far as is necessary to enable that duty or requirement to be performed or complied with.
refs: HSWA 1974 s.8 (duty not to interfere with or misuse things provided for safety)
HSWA 1974 s.36 (offences due to fault of other person)

sections.title: Risk assessment — Management of Health and Safety at Work Regulations 1999
sections.summary: Every employer must make a suitable and sufficient assessment of the risks to the health and safety of their employees and of persons not in their employment arising out of or in connection with their undertaking. The assessment must be reviewed if there is reason to suspect it is no longer valid or there has been a significant change. Employers with 5 or more employees must record the significant findings and any group of employees identified as being especially at risk. Risk assessment is the foundation of the regulatory framework — it drives all subsequent control measures, training, monitoring, and review. Special risk assessments are required for new and expectant mothers (reg.16), young persons (reg.19), and specific hazards such as manual handling, DSE, COSHH, noise, and vibration.
sections.text:
(1) Every employer shall make a suitable and sufficient assessment of—
(a) the risks to the health and safety of his employees to which they are exposed whilst they are at work; and
(b) the risks to the health and safety of persons not in his employment arising out of or in connection with the conduct by him of his undertaking,
for the purpose of identifying the measures he needs to take to comply with the requirements and prohibitions imposed upon him by or under the relevant statutory provisions.

(3) Any assessment such as is referred to in paragraph (1) or (2) shall be reviewed by the employer or self-employed person who made it if—
(a) there is reason to suspect that it is no longer valid; or
(b) there has been a significant change in the matters to which it relates;
and where as a result of any such review changes to an assessment are required, the employer or self-employed person concerned shall make them.

(6) Where the employer employs five or more employees, he shall record—
(a) the significant findings of the assessment; and
(b) any group of his employees identified by it as being especially at risk.

versions.description: Original Management of Health and Safety at Work Regulations 1992 (SI 1992/2051) introduced the risk assessment obligation, implementing EU Framework Directive 89/391/EEC.

Replaced by Management of Health and Safety at Work Regulations 1999 (SI 1999/3242). Extended and clarified the risk assessment requirements. Added specific requirements for new and expectant mothers and young persons.

leadingCases.name: Allison v London Underground Ltd [2008] ICR 636
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal considered whether an employer's failure to carry out a suitable and sufficient risk assessment for workplace stress constituted a breach of MHSWR reg.3. The court held that the obligation to assess psychosocial risks is part of the general risk assessment duty and that stress-related risks must be assessed alongside physical risks.
leadingCases.significance: Confirmed that workplace stress and psychosocial risks fall within the scope of the general risk assessment obligation

refs: HSWA 1974 s.2 (employer's general duty — risk assessment evidences compliance)
MHSWR 1999 reg.16 (risk assessment for new and expectant mothers)
MHSWR 1999 reg.19 (risk assessment for young persons)
Manual Handling Operations Regulations 1992 reg.4 (specific risk assessment for manual handling)
Health and Safety (Display Screen Equipment) Regulations 1992 reg.2 (DSE workstation assessment)
Control of Substances Hazardous to Health Regulations 2002 reg.6 (COSHH assessment)
HSE Approved Code of Practice: Management of Health and Safety at Work (L21)

sections.title: Reporting of Injuries, Diseases and Dangerous Occurrences Regulations 2013
sections.summary: Employers must report certain workplace injuries, diseases and dangerous occurrences to the HSE or local authority. Reportable incidents include: deaths, specified injuries (fractures other than fingers/thumbs/toes, amputations, permanent loss of sight, crush injuries, burns covering more than 10% of the body, scalping, loss of consciousness, hypothermia/heat-induced illness requiring hospitalisation), over-7-day incapacitation (employee unable to work for more than 7 consecutive days), occupational diseases (carpal tunnel syndrome, tendonitis, occupational dermatitis, hand-arm vibration syndrome, occupational asthma, mesothelioma, and others), and dangerous occurrences (collapse of scaffolding, failure of load-bearing equipment, explosion, release of flammable substances, etc.). Reports must be made without delay for deaths and specified injuries, and within 15 days for over-7-day injuries. Records must be kept for at least 3 years.
sections.text:
Under RIDDOR 2013, employers must report:

• Deaths: any death arising from a work-related accident must be reported immediately to the relevant enforcing authority
• Specified injuries: fractures (other than fingers, thumbs, toes), amputations, permanent loss of sight, crush injuries to the head or torso, burns/scalds covering more than 10% of the body or causing significant damage, scalping, loss of consciousness caused by head injury or asphyxia, hypothermia or heat-induced illness requiring hospitalisation for more than 24 hours
• Over-7-day incapacitation: where an employee is unable to perform their normal work duties for more than 7 consecutive days
• Non-fatal accidents to non-workers: where a person not at work (e.g. a visitor or member of the public) is taken directly to hospital for treatment
• Occupational diseases: including carpal tunnel syndrome, severe cramp of the hand or forearm, occupational dermatitis, hand-arm vibration syndrome, occupational asthma, tendonitis or tenosynovitis, mesothelioma, and others listed in Schedule 2
• Dangerous occurrences: events listed in Schedule 2 including collapse of scaffolding, crane collapse, explosion, electrical short circuit causing fire, release of biological agents, malfunction of breathing apparatus, and others

Reports are made online at the HSE RIDDOR reporting website. Deaths and specified injuries must be reported without delay (telephone: 0345 300 9923). Over-7-day injuries must be reported within 15 days.

versions.description: Original RIDDOR 1995 (SI 1995/3163) replaced RIDDOR 1985. Required reporting of over-3-day incapacitation.

RIDDOR 2013 (SI 2013/1471) replaced RIDDOR 1995. Changed over-3-day reporting to over-7-day reporting. Simplified the list of reportable dangerous occurrences and occupational diseases. Moved to online reporting.

refs: HSWA 1974 s.33 (offences — failure to report is a criminal offence)

sections.title: HSE inspectors: powers of entry, improvement notices, prohibition notices
sections.summary: HSE inspectors have extensive enforcement powers. They may enter premises at any reasonable time (or at any time if there is a situation of serious danger), examine and investigate, take measurements, photographs, and samples, require production of documents, and question any person. Inspectors may serve improvement notices (requiring the employer to remedy a contravention within a specified period) or prohibition notices (requiring the employer to immediately cease an activity that involves a risk of serious personal injury). Failure to comply with a notice is a criminal offence. Appeals against notices are to an employment tribunal. The HSE may also bring criminal prosecutions for health and safety offences. Penalties include unlimited fines for organisations and, for individuals (including directors under HSWA s.37), custodial sentences of up to two years. The Corporate Manslaughter and Corporate Homicide Act 2007 provides for prosecution of organisations where gross management failures cause death.
sections.text:
HSE inspectors have powers to:

• enter any premises at any reasonable time (or at any time if there is danger)
• take a police officer if there is reasonable cause to apprehend serious obstruction
• examine and investigate as necessary
• require premises to be left undisturbed for examination or investigation
• take measurements, photographs, recordings
• take samples of articles, substances, or the atmosphere
• require any person to answer questions and sign a declaration of truth
• require production of, inspect, and take copies of documents
• seize and render harmless any article or substance which is a cause of imminent danger

Enforcement actions:
• Improvement Notice: requires the employer to remedy a contravention within a specified period (minimum 21 days for appeal)
• Prohibition Notice: requires the employer to immediately cease an activity that involves, or will involve, a risk of serious personal injury. May take immediate effect or be deferred.
• Prosecution: criminal proceedings for health and safety offences. Unlimited fines. Individuals may face custodial sentences up to 2 years.
• Fee for Intervention (FFI): since October 2012, employers who are found to be in material breach of health and safety law must pay the HSE's costs for the time spent identifying and helping to put right the breach (currently £166 per hour).

versions.description: Original enforcement powers established by HSWA 1974 Part I ss.20–25. Created the modern inspection and enforcement framework.
Fee for Intervention scheme introduced, requiring employers in material breach to pay HSE's regulatory costs.

Health and Safety (Offences) Act 2008 (commencement delayed to 2009) and subsequent amendments raised maximum penalties: unlimited fines for all health and safety offences on conviction. Custodial sentences of up to 2 years for certain offences.

leadingCases.name: R v Howe & Son (Engineers) Ltd [1999] 2 All ER 249
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal set out sentencing guidelines for health and safety offences, emphasising that fines should be sufficient to bring home to shareholders and directors the need for a safe environment. The gravity of the offence and the defendant's means are the primary considerations.
leadingCases.significance: Established the sentencing framework for health and safety offences — fines must be meaningful relative to the organisation's means

refs: HSWA 1974 s.37 (offences by bodies corporate — director and officer liability)
Corporate Manslaughter and Corporate Homicide Act 2007 (organisational liability for death caused by gross management failure)
Sentencing Council: Health and Safety Offences, Corporate Manslaughter and Food Safety and Hygiene Offences Definitive Guideline (2016)

pendingAmendments: Post-Brexit — HSWA 1974 and MHSWR 1999 are domestic legislation (not affected by REUL)
HSE enforcement priorities reviewed annually — monitoring
No ERA 2025 amendments to H&S framework directly

parliamentaryOrigin.statutoryBasis: HSE guidance supporting the Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974 (HSWA 1974) and the Management of Health and Safety at Work Regulations 1999 (MHSWR 1999). The guidance covers the employer's general duties under HSWA 1974 s.2-9 and the management duties under MHSWR 1999.
parliamentaryOrigin.legalEffect: Non-statutory guidance. The duties in HSWA 1974 and MHSWR 1999 are legally binding; the guidance provides practical compliance methodology. HSE Approved Codes of Practice (ACOPs) have special legal status under HSWA s.17.
parliamentaryOrigin.note: This guidance file covers the employer's core health and safety duties — the general duty (s.2), duty to non-employees (s.3), risk assessment (MHSWR reg.3), health and safety policy (s.2(3)), information/instruction/training/supervision (s.2(2)(c)), consultation with employees (Safety Representatives and Safety Committees Regulations 1977 / Health and Safety (Consultation with Employees) Regulations 1996).

precedingDocuments.title: Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974
precedingDocuments.significance: The parent Act. See hswa1974.json for full analysis.

precedingDocuments.title: Robens Report: Safety and Health at Work (Cmnd 5034)
precedingDocuments.significance: The Robens Committee report that recommended the framework enacted as HSWA 1974.

leadingCases.name: R v Chargot Ltd [2008] UKHL 73
leadingCases.heldText: The employer must demonstrate adequate management arrangements to discharge the duty of reasonable practicability.

leadingCases.name: R v Nelson Group Services (Maintenance) Ltd [1999] 1 WLR 1526
leadingCases.heldText: An employer's duty under HSWA s.3 extends to persons not in their employment. The duty is not limited to the employer's own employees.
leadingCases.significance: Scope of s.3 duty to non-employees.

leadingCases.name: Kennedy v Cordia (Services) LLP [2016] UKSC 6
leadingCases.heldText: Inadequate risk assessment is strong evidence of breach of duty.
leadingCases.significance: Supreme Court — risk assessment as evidence of compliance.
